summary: Gruber responds to a Washington Post poll showing 59% of Americans support the CIA's harsh interrogation techniques, finding the result disappointing but unsurprising. He links to a cartoon illustrating American hypocrisy on torture and argues that many Americans believe U.S. global leadership entitles the country to actions it would condemn in others. He highlights a Fox News host's reaction as perfectly encapsulating the flawed logic: if the U.S. is inherently good, then its actions must be justified. Gruber argues this reasoning is backwards — a nation's morality is defined by its actions, not assumed in advance.
key_points: A 2-to-1 majority of Americans supported CIA torture methods despite half acknowledging the treatment constituted torture
American exceptionalism leads many to accept conduct from the U.S. that they would never tolerate from other nations
The Fox News host's 'we are awesome' defense perfectly illustrates circular moral reasoning — assuming goodness to justify any action
Gruber argues morality is determined by actions, not by national identity or prior assumption of righteousness
money_quotes: There are many Americans who see the United States's role as the leading nation of the western world as entitling us to do things we'd never tolerate if done by others.
What I would prefer would be to see the United States lead by example — to be the last nation to torture prisoners.

I think Tantaros perfectly explained why so many Americans think they support torture: if you start with the assumption that the U.S. is morally good, then whatever our government did must have been morally justified.
That's the thinking. Alas, that's backwards.
Our morality is based on our actions, not the other way around.
On the one side: Do unto others as you would have them do unto you. On the other: Do unto others before they do unto you. Only one of those mindsets is "good".
key_insight: A nation's moral standing is earned through its actions, not assumed as a premise to justify whatever those actions happen to be.
